stem: In the expansion of \( \left( x + \dfrac{[[c]]}{x} \right)^{[[n]]} \), what is the term that does not depend on \( x \) (the constant term)?
solution: The general term is \( \binom{[[n]]}{j}\, x^{[[n]]-j} \left(\dfrac{[[c]]}{x}\right)^{j} = \binom{[[n]]}{j} ([[c]])^{j}\, x^{[[n]]-2j} \). It is independent of \( x \) when \( [[n]] - 2j = 0 \), i.e. \( j = [[h]] \), giving \( \binom{[[n]]}{[[h]]} ([[c]])^{[[h]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is only the binomial coefficient \( \binom{n}{n/2} \); it omits the factor \( c^{n/2} \).
This raises \( c \) to the power \( n \) instead of \( n/2 \).
This is off by one from the correct constant term.
This is off by one from the correct constant term.
This is off by two from the correct constant term.

stem: In the expansion of \( \left( x^{[[p]]} + \dfrac{[[c]]}{x^{[[q]]}} \right)^{[[n]]} \), what is the term that does not depend on \( x \) (the constant term)?
solution: The general term is \( \binom{[[n]]}{k} (x^{[[p]]})^{[[n]]-k} \left(\dfrac{[[c]]}{x^{[[q]]}}\right)^{k} = \binom{[[n]]}{k} ([[c]])^{k}\, x^{[[p]]\cdot[[n]] - ([[p]]+[[q]])k} \). It is independent of \( x \) when \( [[p]]\cdot [[n]] = ([[p]]+[[q]])k \), i.e. \( k = [[j]] \), giving \( \binom{[[n]]}{[[j]]} ([[c]])^{[[j]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This omits the factor \( c^{k} \) from the term.
This uses the wrong term index, \( n-j \) instead of \( j \), swapping which factor gets the exponent \( k \).
This is off by one from the correct constant term.
This is off by one from the correct constant term.
This is off by two from the correct constant term.

stem: For how many positive integers \( n \) with \( 2 \le n \le [[N]] \) does the expansion of \( \left( x + \dfrac{1}{x} \right)^{n} \) have a term that does not depend on \( x \)?
solution: A constant term exists exactly when \( n \) is even (so that \( j = n/2 \) is a whole number). The even integers from 2 to \( [[N]] \) number \( \lfloor [[N]]/2 \rfloor = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This counts every integer from 2 to \( N \), not just the even ones.
This over-counts the even integers in the range by one.
This under-counts the even integers in the range by one.
This rounds up instead of down, which over-counts when \( N \) is odd.
This is larger than the correct count.
This is smaller than the correct count.
